data.title: Droneshield Contracts Leave Bull Optimistic on Customer ActivityMarket Talk
data.description: Droneshield's latest contract announcement gives the defense tech provider's bull at Bell Potter confidence that a lull in customer activity is ending. Analyst Daniel Laing writes in a note that activity appears to be increasing following a pause linked to last year's U.S. election. He points out that Droneshield's latest package of five standalone repeat contracts means it has at least A$84 million in contracted revenue for delivery in 2025, compared with A$57 million in 2024 revenue. He sees Droneshield benefiting from macro tailwinds, not least the Trump administration's prioritization of drones. Bell Potter keeps a buy rating on the stock and raises its target price 18% to A$1.30. Shares are up 11% at A$1.1475. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Foxconn Industrial Internet to Buy Back Shares Worth Up to 1 Billion Yuan
data.description:
Hon Hai Precision unit Foxconn Industrial Internet will repurchase shares worth between 500 million yuan and 1 billion yuan.

The shares will be bought back at up to 20 yuan apiece and the repurchase will be executed via centralized bidding, according to a Monday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The computer and telecommunications equipment manufacturer will cancel the shares repurchased to reduce registered capital.

The repurchase will be funded internally and completed within 12 months upon shareholder approval, it added.

data.title: Wenling MCT Chairman/CEO Resigns
data.description:
Wenling Zhejiang Measuring and Cutting Tools Trading Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Pan Haihong has resigned effective Monday, according to a same-day filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The cutting tools company has not yet named Pan's successor.

Shares in Wenling MCT fell nearly 5% during late morning trading on Tuesday.

data.title: China, Hong Kong shares flat; losses in chip stocks offset banks' gains
data.description:
China and Hong Kong stocks were flat on Tuesday, as investors awaited more clarity on the trade situation after the Trump administration stepped up probes into semiconductor imports.

** China's blue-chip CSI300 Index and the Shanghai Composite Index both declined less than 0.1% by midday trading break.

** Hong Kong benchmark Hang Seng added less than 0.2% after swinging between gains and losses during the morning session.

** The U.S. Donald Trump administration has escalated probes into semiconductor imports on national security grounds after exempting some Chinese tech products from steep tariffs.

** The ongoing trade uncertainty weighed on chip stocks for both onshore and offshore markets. The CSI Semiconductor Industry Index (.CSI931865) slid 1.7% and the electronics subindex (.CSI930652) lost 1.5%, both giving up most of the gains made on Monday.

** The Hang Seng Tech Index lost 0.9% in Hong Kong, with electric vehicle (EV) maker Xpeng declining 3.5% and chip maker SMIC weakening 3.2%.

** "The U.S.–China tariff situation is highly fluid, with changes occurring almost by the hour," Yan Wang, chief emerging markets and China strategist at Alpine Macro, said in a note.

** There is still heightened uncertainty in the near term, so aggressive risk-taking in Chinese stocks is not warranted, he added.

** On the other hand, the CSI Banks Index (.CSI399986) added 1.4% and consumer staple index gained 0.4%, helping offset losses in the mainland market.

data.title: Philippines Remittances Up 2.7% YoY in February
data.description:
Cash remittances coursed through banks in the Philippines grew by 2.7% year-on-year to reach USD 2.72 billion in February 2025 from USD 2.65 billion in the corresponding month of the previous year.

The rise was mainly attributed to higher receipts from both land-based (3.0%) and sea-based (1.2%) workers.

In the January to February period, cash remittances rose 2.8% to USD 5.63 billion, driven by increased remittances from the United States, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, and the United Arab Emirates.

Meanwhile, in terms of country sources, the United States accounted for the largest share of 40.9%, followed by Singapore (7.6%), Saudi Arabia (6.0%), Japan (5.2%), and United Kingdom (4.8%).

data.title: How do CSL, ResMed, Cochlear and Ansell fare against Trump tariffs?
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump's universal 10% tariffs will impact healthcare names like Ansell and ResMed, while Cochlear, Ramsay and Sonic Healthcare face no direct exposure
  • Ansell may need a 6-7% price hike to offset tariff costs, ResMed 2-3%, and CSL and Fisher & Paykel about 1%, with Ansell most vulnerable due to Asian manufacturing reliance
  • CSL and ResMed are Morgan Stanley’s top picks, favored for strong earnings growth, low volatility, and attractive valuations
  • Recent share price trends show Ansell down 12% in a month, while Fisher & Paykel gained 4%, reflecting tariff-related pressures

Australian healthcare stocks face a challenging landscape due to new tariffs under the Trump administration, with Morgan Stanley highlighting CSL and ResMed as the best equipped to handle these risks.

The Trump administration recently introduced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs, resetting the rate for most countries (except China) to a universal 10%. This shift directly impacts healthcare companies, with varying degrees of exposure:

  • Ansell : Manufacturing sites in Sri Lanka, Malaysia, India, Thailand, and China are subject to US tariffs.

  • Cochlear : Expected to be exempt from tariffs as part of the US Harmonized Tariff Schedule.

  • CSL : Pharmaceuticals currently excluded from US reciprocal tariffs, but an update on pharmaceuticals is expected soon.

  • Fisher & Paykel : Mexican manufacturing exempt from tariffs via the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA). However, New Zealand manufacturing faces a 10% tariff.

  • Ramsay Health Care : No direct impact from US tariffs.

  • ResMed : Ex-US manufacturing in Australia, Singapore, and Malaysia faces a 10% tariff. The company has not provided any commentary on potential exemptions.

  • Sonic Healthcare : No direct impact from US tariffs.

To counter the financial strain of these tariffs, Morgan Stanley estimates Ansell would need to hike prices by 6% to 7%, ResMed by 2% to 3%, and both CSL and FPH by about 1%. These relatively small adjustments suggest most companies can manage the impact, though Ansell’s heavy reliance on Asian manufacturing makes it more vulnerable.

Interestingly, the financial impact is aligned with recent share price performance. Over the past month, Ansell has underperformed with a 12% dip, while Fisher & Paykel leads the pack with a 4.0% gain.

ASX healthcare share price performance, with Fisher & Paykel (black), Cochlear (yellow), Sonic Healthcare (teal), Ramsay Health Care (blue), CSL (red), ResMed (orange) and Annsell (green) |Source: TradingView

Performance During the Global Financial Crisis

The broader economic outlook adds another layer of uncertainty, with Trump’s tariffs fueling fears of a global slowdown. Analysts from Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan and even betting sites like Polymarket see the probability of a US recession by year-end between 30% and 52%.

This has driven investors toward defensive sectors, with only three S&P/ASX 200 sectors — Telecommunications, Staples, and Industrials — showing gains year-to-date.

Reflecting on the Global Financial Crisis from December 2007 to June 2009, Australian healthcare stocks demonstrated resilience. While the S&P/ASX 200 fell 38%, the healthcare sector dropped just 15%, outperforming the market by 23%.

GFC Performance Across Three Charts

Morgan Stanley’s analysis shows that five of the six covered companies, excluding Fisher & Paykel, posted earnings growth in 2008–09. Fisher & Paykel, despite a dip in FY08, rebounded sharply the following year.

Source: Morgan Stanley Research

Source: Morgan Stanley Research

During this period, Ramsay Health Care was the only stock to rise, while Ansell, Sonic Healthcare, and Cochlear faced the steepest declines.

Source: Morgan Stanley Research

Preferred healthcare picks

Amid this backdrop, Morgan Stanley favors CSL and ResMed as the top healthcare picks. Both companies show strong earnings growth, minimal earnings volatility, and appealing valuations compared to their 10-year averages.

data.title: Yunnan Tin Foresees Up to 62% Rise in Q1 Profit
data.description:
Yunnan Tin forecasts its profit to increase 44% to 62% to between 470 million yuan and 530 million yuan in the first quarter from 326.3 million yuan in the year-ago period, according to a Tuesday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

Earnings per share at the tin company are seen to increase to between 0.2856 yuan and 0.3320 yuan from 0.1982 yuan a year earlier.

Yunnan Tin attributed the upbeat forecast to higher prices of tin, copper, zinc, and other materials, among other factors, the filing said.

data.title: Century Pacific Food's Earnings Growth Trajectory Looks SustainableMarket Talk
data.description: Century Pacific Food's earnings growth trajectory looks sustainable, Maybank Securities' Daphne Sze says in a research report. The company could generate topline growth of 7% this year, supported by its branded foods business as original equipment manufacturers' exports may slow given rising skipjack tuna prices, the analyst says. The company has also benefited from the Philippines' mid-term election spending and improving consumer sentiment in 1Q. However, the brokerage trims its 2025 and 2026 earnings forecasts for the Philippine company by 7% and 4%, respectively, to reflect rising tuna and milk prices. It lowers the stock's target price of PHP45.00 versus PHP47.00 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 2.2% lower at PHP36.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Indian bond yields lower on RBI's surprise liquidity move
data.description:
Indian government bond yields slipped early on Tuesday after the country's central bank surprised markets with a plan to inject additional liquidity, aiming to maintain a surplus and support rate cut transmission.

The 10-year yield benchmark yield (IN067934G=CC) was at 6.4135% as of 10:15 a.m., compared with its previous close of 6.4445%.

The 10-year bond yield hit 6.4023% in opening trades, its lowest since December 20, 2021, after the Reserve Bank of India announced a bond purchase of 400 billion rupees ($4.67 billion) and conducted a 43-day repo for 1.5 trillion rupees last Thursday.

Sentiment was buoyed by the RBI's move to maintain surplus liquidity, which stood at 1.69 trillion rupees as of Friday and has remained positive since end-March 2025.

Indian bond markets were shut on Monday for a local holiday.

The RBI, which cut rates by 25 bps and shifted to an accommodative stance last week, surprised markets by announcing another bond purchase outside its April schedule.

It had earlier signalled it would maintain liquidity at around 1% of deposits.

Investors are also awaiting India's retail inflation data due later on Tuesday for further cues on the rate cycle.

India's retail inflation likely held steady in March at 3.60%, just below February's 3.61%, according to a Reuters poll of 40 economists.

Bets of continued lower inflation readings has led some economists to expect a deeper-than-expected rate-cut cycle.

RATES

The one-year swap (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was not yet traded, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) was down nearly 2 basis points to 5.69%.

India's overnight index swap rates are expected to decline further amid the RBI's liquidity push and rate easing cycle, traders have said.

($1 = 85.7050 Indian rupees)

data.title: India stocks, rupee, swaps, call at 10:15 a.m. IST
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex was up 2% to 76754, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 rose 2.1% to 23314, tracking global gains as U.S. President Donald Trump exempted electronics from steep "reciprocal" tariffs.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee rose 0.4% versus the U.S. dollar to 85.6725, boosted by a broadly weaker greenback and strong interbank dollar sales.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN067934G=CC) was quoted at 102.655 rupees, with the yield down 3 bps at 6.4114% after the central bank surprised the market by announcing further liquidity injections, traders say.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was flat at 5.775%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) declined nearly 2 bps to 5.69%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5.85%, and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 5.74%.

data.title: Amaranatha Atla Recognized with a 2025 Global Recognition Award for Excellence in IT and Banking Technologies
data.description:
NEW YORK, April 15, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Amaranatha Atla, a technology leader, has been honored with a 2025 Global Recognition Award for outstanding contributions to IT and banking technologies, marking a significant milestone in a distinguished career spanning over two decades of innovation excellence. The award recognizes Atla's exceptional ability to bridge technical innovation with practical business applications in high-stakes banking environments where security, compliance, and performance are paramount. Industry experts have noted how Atla's implementations have become global reference architectures for financial institutions worldwide, influencing broader sector practices and establishing new benchmarks for technological integration.

The recognition acknowledges Atla's remarkable technical expertise in Java J2EE and Microservices development, combined with effective leadership consistently delivering innovative technological solutions. Banking industry specialists consistently highlight how these implementations have addressed complex challenges while maintaining the highest quality standards in environments where regulatory requirements create significant hurdles. This achievement reflects numerous accomplishments spanning multiple financial technology domains, from system architecture to talent development.

Technical Innovation Excellence

Atla's expertise with complex Java J2EE frameworks, Spring, Hibernate, and Microservices Architecture has laid the foundation for numerous modern financial systems deployed across international markets. This technical knowledge translates into practical implementation, supporting successful cloud migration initiatives utilizing AWS and Azure platforms that have significantly improved how banking institutions deploy and scale their digital infrastructure. Organizations using Atla's architectural designs report substantial improvements in system performance, security posture, and adaptability to market conditions, directly enhancing customer experiences and operational efficiencies.

"My approach has always been to develop solutions that meet stringent compliance standards such as PSD2 and GDPR and enhance performance metrics while improving user experience across multiple touchpoints," Atla explained. According to industry forecasts for 2025, financial institutions that adopt similar integration approaches are projected to see a 27 percent increase in operational efficiency and customer satisfaction scores. These innovations have achieved high adoption rates and consistently positive user feedback. This shows the practical impact of Atla's technical vision while creating measurable business value for financial institutions managing digital transformation challenges.

Leadership and Global Influence

Colleagues describe Atla's leadership style as strategic and pragmatic, carefully balancing innovation with achievable outcomes that deliver tangible business value. Organizations guided by Atla have adopted technological evolution and continuous improvement methodologies. This has helped create environments where innovation succeeds without compromising operational stability or compliance requirements. Mentorship represents an area where Atla has made significant contributions, creating innovative knowledge transfer methods that have improved learning outcomes for junior developers and technology professionals across multiple organizations.

Atla's work demonstrates a deep understanding of how financial technology functions across diverse markets and regulatory environments that differ significantly by region. Technical frameworks designed by Atla have been successfully implemented across multiple continents, showing versatility while maintaining core architectural integrity and security standards regardless of location. "Banking technology requires more than technical proficiency—it demands understanding financial operations, regulatory requirements, security concerns, and customer expectations," Atla noted. "Throughout my career, I've worked to develop solutions that address these complex interactions while building foundations that can adapt to future innovations."

The Global Recognition Award Selection Committee unanimously agreed that Atla exemplifies technical excellence combined with effective leadership in a sector where these qualities must exist together for successful outcomes. Industry practitioners often reference Atla's mission-critical implementations as valuable case studies in modernizing legacy banking systems without disrupting essential financial services. Financial technology education programs have incorporated case studies based on Atla's implementations, helping future banking technology professionals learn from the architectural approaches developed throughout Atla's distinguished career.

Alex Sterling from the Global Recognition Awards remarked: "Amaranatha Atla combines deep technical expertise with a strategic vision that advances industry practices. Our committee was impressed by the innovative solutions developed and how these implementations have become important reference points for financial institutions undertaking digital transformation. Atla's commitment to mentorship ensures these contributions will continue to influence banking technology for years to come, creating an impact that extends beyond specific implementations to include generations of IT professionals who continue to apply the methodologies and innovative approaches they learned under Atla's guidance."

About Global Recognition Awards

Global Recognition Awards is an international organisation that acknowledges outstanding companies and individuals who have significantly contributed to their industries.

Contact Information:

Spokesperson: Alex Sterling

Name of Company: Global Recognition Awards

Website: https://globalrecognitionawards.org/

Email Address: alex@globalrecognitionawards.org

A photo accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/60c272d3-9587-43c8-a924-928f904f2acd

www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/a575dbdf-e3ba-4f5b-b813-b3f465ecdcd1

Amaranatha Atla Recognized with a 2025 Global Recognition Award for Excellence in IT and Banking Technologies

Photo Courtesy of Amaranatha Atla

data.title: G7 finance ministers, central bank governors to hold meeting in the US - report
data.description: This according to Kyodo News. This will definitely be an interesting one to watch out for when it happens. But unless Trump is also involved in the meeting, I reckon it might not lead to much. The meeting is expected to take place in Washington.

data.title: Collins Foods Has Options for Taco Bell ExitMarket Talk
data.description: Fast-food operator Collins Foods has several ways to pursue its exit from Taco Bell in Australia, Barrenjoey analyst Peter Marks writes in a note. Marks tells clients that management is considering transferring operations to U.S.-listed brand owner Yum Brands. He says the other options are to find another owner, close stores one by one, or shut the whole chain in a single move. He suggests that some stores could be converted to the KFC brand, which Collins also operates in Australia, but notes that many stores are too close to existing KFC outlets. Barrenjoey has a neutral rating and a A$9.00 target price on the stock, which is down 5.8% at A$8.165. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: White House to Launch Fed Chair Selection Process This Fall
data.description:
The White House will begin interviewing candidates this fall to potentially succeed Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, whose term ends in May 2026, according to U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Monday.

Bessent expressed no concern about President Trump removing Powell or undermining the Fed's independence.

However, he highlighted the need for a broader discussion regarding the central bank's regulatory responsibilities. “I’ve repeatedly said the Fed has two duties, and I believe that monetary policy is a jewel box that’s got to be preserved.

And then they have regulatory policies.

And I think we can have more of a discussion,” he said, pointing out that the Fed is just one of three main bank regulators, alongside the Comptroller of the Currency and the FDIC.

data.title: Wangsu Science & Technology's 2024 Profit Rises 10%
data.description:
Wangsu Science & Technology's attributable profit rose 10% to 674.5 million yuan in 2024 from 613.1 million yuan in 2023, according to an annual report filed Tuesday with the Hong Kong bourse.

Earnings per share at the Chinese internet data center company increased 8% to 0.27 yuan from 0.25 yuan in the previous year.

Operating income grew 4.8% to 4.93 billion yuan from 4.71 billion yuan a year earlier.

data.title: Australia's Hub24 falls, Morningstar flags overvaluation and tariff uncertainty
data.description:
** Shares of Hub24 fall 3.2% to A$65.27

** Data solutions provider reports total funds under administration of A$124.1 billion as of March end

** Morningstar says despite solid flows in line with expectations, HUB faces challenges from U.S. tariff-related uncertainties and from fast-growing peer Netwealth

** Says that market is overvaluing HUB, pricing in aggressive assumptions about growth in flows and margins, while underestimating significant investment required for flow wins

** Flags unrealistic expectations to justify current prices, as it would require lowering cost of capital to ~5%

** Lifts fair value estimate to A$31 from A$30

** Stock down 6.6% YTD

data.title: RattanIndia's Revolt Motors Opens First Showroom in Nepal
data.description:
Revolt Motors, the electric motorcycle brand of RattanIndia Enterprises , has forayed into the Nepal market with the launch of its first dealership in Kathmandu, according to a Tuesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Revolt's entry into Nepal is backed by its strategic partnership with MV Dugar Group. Both companies plan to open 12 more showrooms in key cities of Nepal.

Shares of the company were up over 2% in recent trade.

data.title: Trump's Tariffs to Likely Target Chips, Displays to Boost U.S. Tech ManufacturingMarket Talk
data.description: Trump's upcoming tech tariffs will likely focus on the semiconductor and display components of imported tech products, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Using iPhone 16 Pro Max as an example, Jefferies estimates that only 22% of its semiconductor and display-related cost is currently sourced from the U.S. Trump's long-term objective is likely to increase that figure to 100%, they say. The analysts note that these contents are currently manufactured by TSMC, Samsung and SK Hynix--expecting Samsung and SK Hynix to raise their U.S. investments, following TSMC's lead. "However, we see any semiconductor tariffs in the above form likely to be implemented on a 'phased-in' basis, with one year of exemption and then escalating over the next 3 years," they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0037 ET - Trump's upcoming tech tariffs will likely focus on the semiconductor and display components of imported tech products, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Using iPhone 16 Pro Max as an example, Jefferies estimates that only 22% of its semiconductor and display-related cost is currently sourced from the U.S. Trump's long-term objective is likely to increase that figure to 100%, they say. The analysts note that these contents are currently manufactured by TSMC, Samsung and SK Hynix--expecting Samsung and SK Hynix to raise their U.S. investments, following TSMC's lead. "However, we see any semiconductor tariffs in the above form likely to be implemented on a 'phased-in' basis, with one year of exemption and then escalating over the next 3 years," they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2351 ET - Biopharmaceutical company CSL and medical equipment firm Resmed have the best combination of attractive valuation and low tariff impact among Australian healthcare stocks, suggests Morgan Stanley equity analyst David L. Bailey. He estimates that CSL would need to raise prices by 1% and Resmed would need to raise prices by 2%-3% to offset the earnings impact from tariffs. But given their solid earnings per share growth, low earnings risk and valuation appeal, he says these companies are the "most favorably positioned" among peers. One word of caution--pharmaceuticals are currently excluded from reciprocal tariffs, but Trump has signaled he wants to put tariffs on that sector soon, which could impact CSL. (mike.cherney@wsj.com; @Mike_Cherney)

1704 ET - A new Canada political poll, issued two weeks prior to the country's April 28 national election, indicates voters' concern over President Trump's trade policy is easing - to the detriment of the incumbent Liberals, which have held a solid lead for most of the campaign. Angus Reid Institute says the Liberals enjoy 45% support versus 39% for the Conservatives, marking a narrower lead. Meanwhile, the poll suggests that 29% of voters identify U.S. relations and Trump trade policy as a top issue, down from a peak of 41% in mid-March. Meanwhile, cost of living and health care are the top two electoral issues, at 56% and 38%. "This race may be shifting slightly," Angus Reid says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1441 ET - Best Buy sales stabilized after jumping 19% during the first week of April, according to D.A. Davidson point of sale data. The bump, which was the highest of any of the past seven weeks, likely reflected consumers trying to buy pricey electronics before tariffs were implemented, D.A. Davidson analyst Michael Baker says. Best Buy sales growth declined to 1.6% the following week. Lowe's and Tractor Supply Co. also saw sales growth slow significantly between the first and second weeks of April, falling 18% and 11% respectively, per the data. For both those companies, the first week of April marked their highest sales growth in the past seven weeks. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1428 ET - While Apple isn't fully out of the woods after Trump announced a tariff exemption for tech products, the worst case scenario of a continuing "tit-for-tat" trade war escalation will likely no longer take place, KeyBanc analysts say in a research note. "We find it difficult to argue for further downside," they say, as they upgrade Apple to sector weight from underweight. But the analysts think consensus expectations are still too high, particularly looking out to FY26, "which call for an accelerating growth profile," they say. Apple rises 3.6% to $205.36. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1422 ET - Oil prices appear to have stabilized following the U.S. "Liberation Day" tariff rollout, but at levels that underscore the market's bearish view on demand, John Coleman of Sparta Commodities says in a note. "Trade war frictions continue to weigh on WTI, as retaliatory tariffs, and likely more policy uncertainty to come, force it to price far more competitively than usual," he says. WTI last week appeared to offer the best relative value globally, but the U.S. benchmark light crude lost some of its competitive edge with a softening in North Sea prices and a "near-collapse of Middle Eastern premia," he adds. WTI is off 0.6% at $61.13 a barrel, and Brent is off 0.4% at $64.52 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1335 ET - General Motors and Ford are in weaker positions than peers due to tariff exposure, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a research note. The auto makers will likely deliver solid 1Q results, but withdraw their full-year guidance as they implement tariff mitigation strategies, the analysts say. "As such, we reluctantly downgrade General Motors to hold from buy given structural uncertainty around U.S. industrial/tariff policy," the analysts say, while also cutting their target price on GM to $43 from $58. "While the stock already trades at a low multiple, we worry it may be an impaired asset for several years," the analysts add. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1326 ET - Crude futures turn lower after a positive start buoyed by U.S. tariff relief for electronics and a reported rise in Chinese oil imports. Oil backed off as equities trimmed some gains, BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says. "I'd say the market lost its luster with the selloff in equities, and there's still a lot of demand concerns in the market because there are still a lot of what ifs and uneasiness over the tariffs," he says. WTI is off 1% at $60.89 a barrel, and Brent is down 0.7% at $64.28 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1223 ET - Apple stock could be seen as a relative safe haven, BofA Securities analysts say in a research note. The iPhone maker is expected to manage its supply chain to minimize the impact of tariffs, the analysts say. If tariffs rates do stand, the analysts would expect an EPS impact to its 2026 results of 41 cents. However, despite the tariff headwinds, analysts would view any potential cut to EPS as relatively muted compared to the potential global disruption that could be caused by an escalating trade war. Shares rise 2.6% to $203.18. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1221 ET - The worst of the Treasury selloff "is probably over," Harbor Capital's Jake Schurmeier says, "absent another big shock." The 10-year last week peaked near 4.5% on Friday and has since declined following a step back on some tariffs. "What you are going to see is a higher risk premium on U.S. assets," Schurmeier says. "In the Treasurys space that is going to be a higher term premium and in the equity space…lower multiples," he says. Schurmeier sees the dwindling demand for U.S. government debt as a long-term trend that may be hard to see in daily trade as clearly as it was observed last week. Yields are declining today, with the 10-year at 4.414%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1206 ET - The tariff exemption for smartphones, PCs, servers and other technology imports from the 145% tariff provides relief for Apple, UBS analysts say in a research note. The iPhone maker would have faced significant economic headwinds that could have reduced its earnings power by 30% given that its supply chain is heavily resilient on China despite its diversification efforts over the past decade, the analysts say. While India has been a growing partner for Apple, this assembly partner could theoretically account for only roughly 10% to 15% of iPhone builds or 25 million to 30 million annually. Overall, UBS no longer expects Apple to raise prices for iPhones in the near term. "However, if tariffs are reinstated at prior levels, we would expect a price increase despite the risk of demand destruction." Shares rise 2.3% to $202.76. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1117 ET - Grains give back some early gains as traders mull the importance to place on President Trump's post on Truth Social on Sunday stating that "NOBODY is getting 'off the hook' [sic]" for tariffs. Friday's news about certain tech products being exempt from tariffs is supporting gains on the stock market, and helped give grain futures a boost in pre-market trade. The USDA reported a new flash sale of 120,000 metric tons of U.S. corn to Japan for delivery in the 2024/25 marketing year this morning, which is a 'positive' for U.S. trader sentiment about foreign demand, Terry Reilly of Marex says. Most-active CBOT corn is down 0.2%, soybeans up 0.5%, and wheat down 1.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Russian GDP Expands 4.5% in Q4
data.description:
Russia’s GDP grew by 4.5% year-on-year in the fourth quarter of 2024, accelerating from an upwardly revised 3.3% in the previous period.

This marks the fastest expansion in three quarters, fueled by wartime spending on the conflict in Ukraine, which has contributed to an overheated economy and pushed annual inflation above 10%.

Sector-wise, growth picked up in wholesale and retail trade (4.9% vs 3.3% in Q3), transportation and storage (2.2% vs 1.5%), ICT (13.7% vs 12.8%), and financial and insurance activities (16.9% vs 16%).

Meanwhile, Russia’s state statistics agency, Rosstat, raised its 2024 GDP growth forecast to 4.3% from 4.1%, citing stronger construction activity, with the sector’s estimate revised up by 2.5 percentage points to 4.2%.
